name: Cat-Cow
description: On hands and knees, alternate arching your spine up toward the ceiling and dropping your belly while looking up. Move with your breath.
whyThisStretch: Mobilizes every vertebra of the spine and warms up the muscles around your lower back without loading them.
whatYouShouldFeel: A gentle wave-like motion through your whole back. No sharp pinches at any point.

educationalCards.title: How it works
educationalCards.body: Cat-Cow alternates between flexion (rounding) and extension (arching). This trains the small muscles between each vertebra to coordinate, restoring range of motion that's lost from sitting all day.

educationalCards.title: Pair it with breath
educationalCards.body: Inhale as you drop your belly into Cow. Exhale as you round into Cat. Match the speed of the movement to the length of your breath.

mistakesToAvoid: Letting your shoulders hike up to your ears — keep them broad and away from the neck.
Cranking your head back at the top — look forward and down, not straight up.
Going too fast. The slower you move, the more vertebrae participate.

name: Child's Pose
description: Kneel, sit back on your heels, and fold forward with arms extended. Rest your forehead on the mat and breathe.
whyThisStretch: A restorative pose that decompresses the lower back and opens the hips while you do almost nothing.
whatYouShouldFeel: A spreading sensation across your low back and the tops of your shoulders. No knee pain.

educationalCards.title: Two variations
educationalCards.body: Knees together compresses the belly and emphasizes the lower back. Knees wide (toes still touching) opens the inner hips and is gentler on the knees.

educationalCards.title: Use it as a reset
educationalCards.body: Return to Child's Pose between any two harder stretches if you need a break. It's effectively impossible to overdo.

mistakesToAvoid: Forcing your forehead to the mat — rest it on a stacked fist or a pillow if it doesn't reach.
Holding tension in your shoulders. Let them drop and melt toward the floor.
Ignoring knee pain. Slide a folded blanket between your hamstrings and calves if your knees complain.

name: Knee to Chest
description: Lying on your back, pull one knee gently toward your chest. Hold, then switch sides.
whyThisStretch: Lengthens the muscles of the lower back and glutes and gently mobilizes the sacroiliac joint.
whatYouShouldFeel: A pull across your low back on the side of the bent knee, plus a stretch in the same-side glute.

educationalCards.title: Why one at a time
educationalCards.body: Single-knee variants keep the other leg lengthened, which keeps tension out of the hip flexor and isolates the lower back better than the double-knee version.

mistakesToAvoid: Yanking the knee — use steady, gentle pressure with your hands.
Letting the lifted shoulder peel off the floor. Keep both shoulder blades grounded.
Holding your breath. Inhale to expand, exhale to draw the knee closer.

name: Supine Spinal Twist
description: Lying on your back, drop both knees to one side while keeping shoulders flat. Hold, then switch.
whyThisStretch: Twists the spine while gravity does most of the work, releasing tension from the lower back and outer hips.
whatYouShouldFeel: A diagonal stretch from the dropped-knee side, across the low back, into the opposite shoulder.

educationalCards.title: The shoulder anchor
educationalCards.body: The far-side shoulder peeling up is the most common compensation. Press it down before the knees drop — that's where the rotational stretch comes from.

educationalCards.title: Knee height matters
educationalCards.body: Higher knees (90/90) emphasize the lumbar spine. Lower knees (closer to straight) emphasize the thoracic spine. Pick the height that targets where you're stiff.

mistakesToAvoid: Forcing the knees all the way to the floor on day one — let them rest on a pillow if they don't reach.
Cranking your head in the opposite direction. Let it follow the spine, not lead.
Bouncing in and out of the twist. Settle once and breathe.

name: Pelvic Tilt
description: Lying on your back with knees bent, flatten your lower back into the floor by tilting your pelvis. Release and repeat.
whyThisStretch: Teaches your deep core to control the pelvis — the prerequisite for almost every safe-back exercise.
whatYouShouldFeel: A gentle pressing of your low back into the floor, driven from the belly, not the glutes.

educationalCards.title: Mostly a coordination drill
educationalCards.body: Pelvic tilts barely look like anything but they're the foundation. Master this motion and stretches like Glute Bridge and Cat-Cow become far more effective.

educationalCards.title: Less is more
educationalCards.body: The motion is small — a couple of centimeters of pelvis tilt is plenty. Big visible movements usually mean you're recruiting the wrong muscles.

mistakesToAvoid: Squeezing the glutes to drive the motion — that's a hip thrust, not a pelvic tilt.
Holding the breath. Exhale as you tilt the pelvis; inhale to release.
Lifting the hips off the floor. Only the pelvis tilts; the seat stays planted.

name: Sphinx Pose
description: Lie on your stomach, prop yourself on your forearms with elbows under shoulders. Lengthen the spine.

name: Seated Forward Fold
description: Sit with legs extended, hinge forward from the hips and reach toward your feet. Keep the spine long.

name: Standing Forward Fold
description: Stand and hinge forward from the hips, letting the upper body hang. Soft knees if needed.

name: Figure-Four Stretch
description: Lying on your back, cross one ankle over the opposite knee and pull the back of the bottom thigh toward you.

name: Pigeon Pose
description: From all fours, bring one shin forward parallel to the front of your mat. Square your hips and fold forward.

name: Low Lunge
description: From a kneeling position, step one foot forward into a deep lunge. Sink the hips and lift the chest.

name: Butterfly Stretch
description: Sit with the soles of your feet together and knees out wide. Hold your feet and gently fold forward.

name: Happy Baby
description: Lying on your back, grab the outsides of your feet with knees wide. Gently rock side to side.

name: Downward-Facing Dog
description: From all fours, tuck the toes and lift the hips up and back into an inverted V.

name: Cobra Pose
description: Lie on your stomach, hands under shoulders, and gently press up to lift the chest off the floor.

name: Wall Hamstring Stretch
description: Lie on your back near a doorway, rest one leg up the wall. Switch legs after the hold.

name: Standing Quad Stretch
description: Stand, grab one ankle, and pull the heel toward your glute. Keep knees together.

name: Wall Calf Stretch
description: Place hands on a wall, step one foot back with the heel pressed down. Keep the back leg straight.

name: Thread the Needle
description: From all fours, slide one arm under the other and rest the shoulder and side of the head on the mat.

name: Glute Bridge
description: Lie on your back with knees bent. Press through your heels to lift your hips, squeeze the glutes, lower.

name: Hip Flexor Lunge
description: Kneel on one knee with the other foot forward. Tuck your pelvis and lean gently into the front leg to feel a stretch in the back hip.

name: Reclined Hand-to-Big-Toe
description: Lying on your back, loop a strap around the ball of one foot and gently straighten the leg toward the ceiling.

name: Standing Side Bend
description: Stand tall, reach one arm overhead, and lean gently to the opposite side. Keep both hips facing forward.

name: Crocodile Pose
description: Lie on your stomach, stack your forearms, and rest your forehead on top. Breathe deeply into your low back.

name: Standing IT Band Stretch
description: Cross one leg behind the other, reach the same-side arm overhead, and lean to the opposite side to open the outer hip.

name: Seated Spinal Twist
description: Sit with one leg crossed over the other. Twist toward the bent knee, lengthening through the spine on each inhale.
